data.title: Cobalt Miners News For The Month Of February 2025
data.description:
Welcome to the February 2025 cobalt miners news.

The past month saw cobalt prices significantly lower, hitting a multi-decade low.

Cobalt Price News

As of February 19, the cobalt spot price was at US$9.77/lb, lower from US$11.02/lb last month. The LME cobalt price is US$20,905/tonne. LME Cobalt inventory is 126 tonnes, flat from 126 tonnes from last month. More details on cobalt pricing (in particular the more relevant cobalt sulphate), can be found here at Benchmark Mineral Intelligence or Fast Markets MB.

Cobalt spot prices long-term price chart - USD 9.77 (Source)

Cobalt Demand Vs. Supply Forecasts

S&P Global Market intelligence - Cobalt demand vs. supply forecast as of Oct. 2024 (Source)

The global cobalt market balance is forecast to shift from surplus to deficit in 2027 (as of June 2024) (Source)

Morgan Stanley forecasts cobalt surpluses to grow this decade (as of Nov. 2023) (Source)

Trend Investing vs. IEA 2021 demand forecast for EV metals

2021 IEA forecast growth in demand for selected minerals from clean energy technologies by scenario, 2040 relative to 2020 - Increases Of Lithium 13x to 42x, Graphite 8x to 25x, Cobalt 6x to 21x, Nickel 7x to 19x, Manganese 3x to 8x, Rare Earths 3x to 7x, And Copper 2x to 3x

Cobalt Market News

On January 30, Miningdotcom reported:

Cobalt price plunge and the EV market...In a recent note, BMO Capital Markets pointed out that in fact real cobalt prices are at the lowest in history based on annual average prices adjusted for inflation going back to the late 1920s using USGS data. Nearly all cobalt output is a byproduct of nickel and copper mining. Copper production in the Congo is rising fast leading to a near 40% jump in the country's cobalt output in 2024. The DRC already accounts for 80% of global cobalt supply, estimated to have exceeded 300,000 tonnes in 2024.

On February 6, Miningdotcom reported:

Column: Another cobalt bust but this time it's different...But while it was an artisanal mining surge that caused the bust of 2018-2019, this time around it's China's giant CMOC Group. The company more than doubled its output of cobalt last year, pumping nearly 60,000 tons of extra metal into a global market of just over 200,000 tons.

On February 14, Fastmarkets reported:

Cobalt market faces oversupply hurdles amid China's demand watch and price challenges...CMOC's guidance for 2025 set at 100-120kt of cobalt suggests we're in for another bearish year for the market. In the US, the potential tariffs on Canadian cobalt metal could see short-term tightening on certain Western brands, however, we're not holding out for a strong recovery in standard-grade cobalt metal pricing for the rest of the year.

Cobalt Company News

CMOC Group Limited [HKSE:3993] [SHE:603993] (OTCPK:CMCLF) (formerly China Molybdenum)

No significant news for the month.

Glencore [HK:805] [LSE:GLEN] (OTCPK:GLCNF)

On January 30, Glencore announced: "Full Year 2024 Production Report." 2024 own sourced cobalt production was 38.2kt down 8% YoY.

On February 19, Glencore announced:

2025 Share buy-back programme. Glencore plc (the "Company") announces the commencement of a programme to make market purchases of its ordinary shares (the "Shares") of an aggregate value of up to USD 1 billion (the "Programme"), subject to market conditions, with completion planned to be by 6 August 2025.

On February 19, Glencore announced:

Preliminary Results 2024..."Our strong operational performance, along with another strong Marketing contribution, supported the generation of Adjusted EBITDA of $14.4 billion and Funds from operations of $10.5 billion during 2024, down 16%, but up 11%, respectively compared to 2023. The decline in Adjusted EBITDA, particularly within the Industrial segment, was mainly a function of lower average energy coal prices year over year...We are pleased to announce c.$2.2 billion (c.$0.182 per share) of shareholder returns in accordance with our capital allocation framework. We are recommending a $0.10 per share (c.$1.2 billion) base cash distribution, together with a 'top-up' buyback of $1.0 billion (c.$0.082 per share)...Net income attributable to equity holders pre-significant items: $3.7 billion; Net loss attributable to equity holders: $1.6 billion.

On February 19, Reuters reported:

Glencore looks at leaving London as shares drop and earnings dip... "Weaker coal prices have been a major headwind for Glencore over the past six months"...The company's net debt rose to $11.2 billion, from $4.9 billion in 2023, partly due to $6.7 billion of net capex and the $7 billion acquisition of metallurgical coal portfolio EVR, which it bought from Teck Resources. This was above analysts' consensus expectation for debt of $8.7 billion.

Chemaf (subsidiary of Shalina Resources) - deal to be taken over by Norin Mining Limited in Q4, 2024, but Congo wants this halted. More recent news here.

Quarterly Activities Report to 31 December 2024. Jervois Finland: 1,308 metric tonnes ("mt") of cobalt sold in Q4 2024 in weak but stable market conditions; total of 5,064 mt for 2024...

On February 20, Electra announced: "Gold-rich cobalt-copper mineralization at CAS Property strengthens Electra's Idaho exploration strategy...Recent and historical data from CAS highlight high-grade gold values over significant intervals...

Alliance Nickel own the NiWest Nickel-Cobalt Project located adjacent to Glencore's Murrin Murrin Nickel operations in the North Eastern Goldfields of Western Australia. The NiWest Project which has an estimated 830,000 tonnes of nickel metal and 52,000 tonnes of cobalt.

On January 31, Alliance Nickel announced:

Conclusion

February saw cobalt spot prices significantly lower and LME inventory levels flat.

Highlights for the month were:

  • Fastmarkets: CMOC's guidance for 2025 set at 100-120kt of cobalt suggests we're in for another bearish year for the market.
  • Glencore 2024 own sourced cobalt production was 38.2kt, down 8% YoY. Announced 2025 up to US$1B share buyback. Weaker coal prices have been a major headwind for Glencore over the past six months.
  • Umicore - Battery Materials: actions taken to counteract headwinds. Launch of a strategic review and a realignment of the workforce as part of the overall cost-saving strategy.
  • Ramu Nickel-Cobalt Mine returns to full production capacity.
  • Jervois Global commences Chapter 11 recapitalisation procedure.
  • Electra - Gold-rich cobalt-copper mineralization at CAS Property strengthens Electra's Idaho exploration strategy.
  • Sunrise Energy Metals - Sunrise Project continued with activities focused on completing studies for the long-lead electrical transmission line etc.
  • Ardea Resources - Significant progress has been made in advancing the KNP - Goongarrie Hub DFS.
  • Cobalt Blue - COB diversifies - Earn-in agreement for major copper project (Halls Creek Project) in WA. Proposes name change to Core Blue Minerals Limited.
  • TMC - World First: TMC and PAMCO achieve a new nodule processing milestone.

As usual, all comments are welcome.

data.title: The State Of REITs: February 2025 Edition
data.description:
REIT Performance

REITs kicked off 2025 with a modestly negative January average total return of -1.29%. REITs fell short of the broader market as the Dow Jones Industrial Average (+4.8%), S&P 500 (+2.8%) and NASDAQ (+1.7%) all saw gains to start the year. The market cap weighted Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) outpaced the average REIT in January (+1.65% vs. -1.29%). The spread between the 2025 FFO multiples of large cap REITs (17.3x) and small cap REITs (12.6x) widened in January, as multiples expanded 0.1 turns for large caps but contracted 0.9 turns for small caps. Investors currently need to pay an average of 37.3% more for each dollar of FFO from large cap REITs relative to small cap REITs. In this monthly publication, I will provide REIT data on numerous metrics to help readers identify which property types and individual securities currently offer the best opportunities to achieve their investment goals.

Micro cap REITs (-7.80%), which severely underperformed their larger peers in 2024, continued their losing streak in January. Large caps (+0.30%) and mid caps (+0.09%) eked out narrow gains, but small caps (-0.95%) averaged a modest negative return. After the first month of 2025, large cap REITs have outperformed small caps by 126 basis points.

7 out of 18 Property Types Yielded Positive Total Returns in January

38.9% of REIT property types averaged a positive total return in January. There was a 15.14% total return spread between the best and worst performing property types. Timber (+7.63%) and Industrial (+4.06%) had the strongest start to 2025. Single Family Housing (-7.51%) and Shopping Center REITs (-6.46%) started the year deep in the red.

The REIT sector as a whole saw the average P/FFO (2025Y) decrease 0.2 turns in January from 13.8x down to 13.6x. 50% of property types averaged multiple contraction, 44.4% averaged multiple expansion and 5.6% saw multiples hold steady in January. Land (32.1x), Data Centers (28.6x), Manufactured Housing (18.8x), Single Family Housing (18.3x) and Multifamily (17.1x) currently trade at the highest average multiples among REIT property types. Hotels (7.2x) and Office (9.1x) are the only property types that average single digit FFO multiples.

Performance of Individual Securities

Medical Properties Trust (MPW) (+18.73%) outperformed all other equity REITs in a very event-filled month for the health care REIT. MPW’s long struggling tenant Prospect Medical Holdings finally filed for bankruptcy on January 11th and on January 31st MPW refinanced large loans that would have matured in 2025 and 2026 with new notes maturing in 2032.

Creative Media & Community Trust (CMCT) (-66.22%) plummeted in January. CMCT announced a 1-for-10 reverse stock split on the first trading day of the year, but the share price continued to rapidly collapse down toward the pre-split price.

42.58% of REITs had a positive total return in January. REITs averaged a -1.29% total return in January 2025, which was actually a less painful start to the year than REITs had the prior January (-5.72%).

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Dividend Yield

Dividend yield is an important component of a REIT's total return. The particularly high dividend yields of the REIT sector are, for many investors, the primary reason for investment in this sector. As many REITs are currently trading at share prices well below their NAV, yields are currently quite high for many REITs within the sector. Although a particularly high yield for a REIT may sometimes reflect a disproportionately high risk, there exist opportunities in some cases to capitalize on dividend yields that are sufficiently attractive to justify the underlying risks of the investment. I have included below a table ranking equity REITs from the highest dividend yield (as of 1/31/2025) to the lowest dividend yield.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Although a REIT’s decision regarding whether to pay a quarterly dividend or a monthly dividend does not reflect on the quality of the company’s fundamentals or operations, a monthly dividend allows for a smoother cash flow to the investor. Below is a list of equity REITs that pay monthly dividends ranked from the highest yield to the lowest yield.

Dividend News

3 REITs announced dividend hikes in January, 2 of which pay quarterly dividends and 1 of which pays monthly. Medalist Diversified REIT (MDRR) (+8.3%) announced the largest dividend increase followed by Postal Realty Trust (PSTL) (+1.0%) and STAG Industrial (STAG) (+0.7%).

A majority (53.9%) of REITs currently pay a higher dividend than they did pre-COVID (the end of 2019). 6.3% have the same dividend they did pre-COVID and 39.8% have a reduced or suspended dividend.

A number of REIT property types saw across the board dividend increases since the end of 2019. 100% of REITs have increased their dividend within the Single-tenant Triple Net, Casino, Land, Self-storage, Data Center, Manufactured Housing and Single Family Housing property types. However, none of the Mall or Outlet Center REITs have a higher dividend today than they did at the end of 2019.

Valuation

REIT Premium/Discount to NAV by Property Type

Below is a downloadable data table, which ranks REITs within each property type from the largest discount to the largest premium to NAV. The consensus NAV used for this table is the average of analyst NAV estimates for each REIT. Both the NAV and the share price will change over time, so I will continue to include this table in upcoming issues of The State of REITs with updated consensus NAV estimates for each REIT for which such an estimate is available.

For the convenience of reading this table in a larger font, the table below is available as a PDF as well.

Takeaway

The large cap REIT premium (relative to small cap REITs) widened in January and investors are now paying on average about 37% more for each dollar of 2025 FFO/share to buy large cap REITs than small cap REITs (17.3x/12.6x - 1 = 37.3%). As can be seen in the table below, there is presently a strong positive correlation between market cap and FFO multiple.

The table below shows the average NAV premium/discount of REITs of each market cap bucket. This data, much like the data for price/FFO, shows a strong, positive correlation between market cap and Price/NAV. The average large cap REIT (-4.80%) and mid cap REIT (-5.92%) trade at mid single-digit discounts to NAV. Small cap REITs (-23.27%) trade at a little over 3/4 of NAV and micro caps (-37.81%) trade at less than 2/3 of their respective NAVs.

Economic Health

2024 had the highest number of corporate bankruptcies in 14 years and January 2025 continued that painful upward trend. January 2025 had twice the number of bankruptcies as January 2024 (70 vs. 35) as high interest rates continue to take a toll on heavily indebted companies.

REITs Overall Saw a Slight Decrease in Short Interest in January, But Short Interest Remains Very High for Some REITs

Average REIT short interest remained largely flat in January as it declined by just 1 basis point to 3.8% of shares outstanding. Advertising REITs continue to average the highest short interest (5.9%) followed by Hotels (5.4%). Timber (1.8%) and Data Centers (1.9%) had the lowest average short interest at the end of January. Shopping Center REITs saw the highest increase in average short interest (+96 bps) in January, whereas Office REITs averaged the largest decrease (-65 bps).

Wheeler REIT (WHLR) saw the biggest increase (+11.2%) in short interest in January as the share price continues a multi-year freefall. Agree Realty (ADC) (+1.7%) and Creative Media & Community Trust (CMCT) (+1.6%) rounded out the top 3 short interest increases. The 10 REITs that saw the biggest declines in short interest were concentrated in 4 property types: Office, Healthcare, Industrial and Diversified. Net Lease Office Properties (NLOP) saw the biggest decline in short interest (-9.7%) followed by Medical Properties Trust (MPW) (-2.4%) and American Healthcare REIT (AHR) (-1.9%).

Closing a short position requires buying back shares in the position that was shorted. This means that companies with very high short interest can potentially experience strong upward pressure on share price when the short thesis materially weakens. A 2.4% reduction in the short interest of MPW helped propel the healthcare REIT to the highest total return in the REIT sector in January. With 35.9% of shares of MPW still held short and a whopping 16.6 days to cover, this heavy short interest could prove to be a strong tailwind if the short thesis fails to play out. Other REITs with high days to cover are Agree Realty Trust (ADC) (15.8 days), Getty Realty (GTY) (15.0 days) and Independence Realty Trust (IRT) (13.4 days). Heavy short interest can often be warranted by material risks and/or declining fundamentals, so it is important to fully understand these issues prior to initiating either a long or short position in any REIT. However, there is a potential for outsized gains when an investor correctly identifies which highly shorted REITs will not see the short thesis come to fruition.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta's Hidden Potential: Time To Upgrade To A Cautious Buy
data.description:
In my last analysis of Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META), I mentioned that the company's fundamentals were very strong, but the overreliance on ads and its social networks made the company less attractive when compared to other big techs like Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL), which are my favorites and have growth supported by the secular cloud trend.

Since then, Meta stock has advanced by 20% and reported a strong Q4. Revisiting the thesis, I believe there is huge potential to be unlocked over the next few years.

However, the lack of visibility in the very long term (more than ten years) still makes it seem that Meta stock has a relatively small margin of safety (when compared to its history or other companies like Google) and is a less safe choice than other big techs, so even with the upgrade to buy, I still reiterate the cautious stance.

Meta’s Huge Potential In Coming Years

Meta's forward price-to-earnings is almost at 28x, while the average of the last 5 years is just under 23x. 28x forward earnings isn't exactly expensive, especially for a company with strong fundamentals like Meta.

The problem is that it will have to maintain the growth we've seen over the last few quarters for a long time, and that will require a mix between organic growth in its core business (advertising, more users on its social platforms, and so on) and the consolidation of other initiatives, such as robotics, VR, and the monetization of WhatsApp.

Starting with its core business, advertising continues to have good momentum, with higher advertiser demand, due to improved ad performance, which allows for a higher price per ad and ends up balancing out the lower YoY growth in ad impressions. These more personalized ads and AI enhancements are very positive for the company and should continue to drive growth in the coming quarters. In the earnings call, the company commented a little on this, dividing “increasing monetization efficiency” into optimizing organic engagement and improving marketing performance:

"The first part of this work is optimizing the level of ads within organic engagement. We continue to grow supply on lower monetizing surfaces, like video, while optimizing ad supply on each of our surfaces to deliver ads at the time and place they will be most relevant to people. For example, we are continuing to better personalize when ads show up, including the optimal locations in the depth of someone’s feed, to introduce ad supply when it’s most optimal for the user and revenue. This is enabling efficient supply growth."

In the long term, they also see some opportunities to boost impression growth with unmonetized surfaces like Threads.

Other growth avenues should also gain traction in the coming years. AI glasses like Ray-Ban Meta AI, Quest, and Horizon, in my view, are a little more nebulous, but they should show some development over the next few quarters, while for others it's possible to be a little more optimistic, such as Meta AI, infrastructure projects, humanoid robots with AI (to basically compete with Tesla's Optimus (TSLA)), other wearable devices, and WhatsApp.

Meta’s margins are also very promising. The company is proving quarter by quarter that it can increase efficiency, with AI helping engineers to be more productive and with even better expectations, such as the expectation of Susan Li, who mentioned that looking forward she hopes that through advancements in Llama's coding capabilities it will be possible to generate code changes to automate tool updates.

The chart below illustrates this trend very well, with a sharp drop in expenses as a percentage of revenue, especially in G&A.

WhatsApp Could Add More Than $10B In Revenue

Just saying that these initiatives, which still account for little (or almost nothing) of Meta's revenue, have growth potential is very intangible. That's why we're going to estimate how much revenue WhatsApp could really generate in a few years if Meta pursues its efforts.

Before starting with the assumptions, it's worth pointing out that this is just an exercise in conjecture, and not an attempt to be precise, since in reality, the company would probably prioritize one initiative and may choose to keep the platform ad-free.

WhatsApp currently has around 2.5B users, most notably in India and Brazil, although it is also gaining ground in the US, surpassing 100 million in the last quarter. Even with so many users, it is estimated that the platform has a revenue of ~$2B per year.

With this 2.5B base, accelerated growth seems very likely. WhatsApp business alone, which is already gaining traction, should be able to add another few billion in revenue over the next few years, but there is still a lot to be explored.

WhatsApp Payments is already a functional feature, but if they really manage to increase adoption, it's possible that there will be an increase in revenue. With $100B of TPV and a 1% fee, it would already be possible to achieve another $1B in revenue. There are, however, some challenges to this. In Brazil, for example, Pix is very popular, making WhatsApp's system practically useless in many cases.

If Meta manages to increase user value to the point where it can charge for a subscription, it could also unlock massive growth. At $1.99/m from 5% of the user base (I estimate ~3B in 2027), it could already add $3.5B in revenue. Of course, for Meta to be able to charge this, it would need to offer some unique value, such as access to a better version of Meta AI.

Some initiative related to e-commerce also seems plausible. Several companies already use WhatsApp to sell, and having a feature integrated into the app could be useful for the merchant, the buyer, and Meta. With a GMV of $5B per year and a take-rate of 5% (conservative figures) we have $250 million in revenue.

Finally, if the company eventually chooses to consider some level of advertising on the platform, this could also be one of the main sources of revenue. It doesn't necessarily have to be an ad that worsens the user experience, it could be a message sent by a company (like LinkedIn does), or in the status, or with some new feature like short videos, live shopping or something similar. Ignoring what these ads would look like, let's estimate that the daily reach would be 1 ad per user, reaching 1 billion people per day, i.e., each user would consume 1 ad every 3 days. If the CPM is $3, that would be enough for a revenue of over $1B.

Putting all these initiatives together and considering a 25% growth in WhatsApp Business Revenue, we have a total revenue of $9.9B in 2027, equivalent to 4% of Meta's revenue that I estimated for this period, and although it's not that relevant, it would already be enough to keep YoY growth at slightly higher levels.

It's worth mentioning that many of the estimates were made conservatively. PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL) charges fees much higher than 1%, and its TPV exceeds $1T. If WhatsApp really manages to get mass adoption of this feature, and they manage to reach $500B TPV eventually, and charge a 2% fee, it would already be an addition of $10B in revenue. However, this already seems like an extremely optimistic and far-fetched scenario.

Similarly, if 10% of the user base (considering 3B) sees value in a WhatsApp subscription, and the price is $2.99/m, there's already $10B in this initiative alone.

If There's So Much Potential, Why Just a “Cautious” Buy for Meta Stock?

When we estimate Meta's fundamentals for the next few years, the asymmetry is still not that great, and as I mentioned earlier, its multiples are above their mean of the last 5 years.

Forecasting 14% revenue growth in 2025 (in line with market estimates), 12% for 2026 and 2027, and then adding WhatsApp's estimated revenue in 2027, we have $245.2B at the end of the period.

Even with my optimism for continued increases in margin through efficiency, it is necessary to be a little more cautious, since in the last quarters, the effective tax rate varied between only 11% and 13%. That's why I assumed a small decline with gradual advances.

With these assumptions, Meta should trade at 20x earnings in 2027, equivalent to an earnings yield of 5%. For a company that has so much potential and is constantly improving both its core and trying to start new ventures, this is a reasonable multiple.

The problem is that it is also necessary to consider that this growth will continue at a minimally attractive pace over the coming decades. Meta continues to increase its DAP quarter by quarter, and recently reached 3.35 billion users. Ad impressions are also no longer growing at an accelerated rate, which leads me to think that in a few years' time, this core growth may slow down, with less room to increase impressions and almost zero growth in new users.

For this reason, the company needs to continue optimizing its ads in order to increase the price (because they are more assertive), as well as maturing other initiatives such as AI and other devices.

The Bottom Line

Even though Meta is not among my favorite Big Techs, like Amazon and Google, the company has managed to prove that it is making significant progress on different fronts, and its fundamentals remain very strong.

Not only that, but there is still massive potential to be explored in its apps, such as WhatsApp which could add a few more billions to revenue in a few years, along with the maturing of new ventures and some optionalities such as the humanoid robots, which have the potential to bring positive surprises.

All this means that the company should unlock significant shareholder value over the next few years and sustain the bull case. However, it is necessary to be cautious due to its multiples being a little higher than historical due to the greater (justified) optimism of the market.

These factors support my upgrade to a “cautious buy” instead of a hold.

data.title: Blue Bird Corporation: An Opportunity To Hitch A Ride To Strong Upside
data.description:
From my observations in the market, I believe one of the most interesting prospects these days for investors to consider is Blue Bird Corporation (NASDAQ:BLBD). The school bus manufacturer is intriguing because of its emphasis moving forward on the production and sale of electric school buses. While this might not seem like the most exciting space for many market participants, the fact of the matter is that management has a pretty good plan for the future and shares look attractively priced.

Lately, performance for the business has not been exactly stellar. Since I last upgraded the stock from a ‘buy’ to a ‘strong buy’ back in November of last year, shares are actually down 12.4% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 1.7%. This comes on the back of relatively weak revenue and cash flows. The backlog has also declined. However, industry conditions favor the business and the price at which shares are trading seems to leave a lot of upsides for shareholders moving forward. Because of this, I have no reason to do anything other than reaffirm the company as a ‘strong buy’ candidate.

Short-term bumps

When I last wrote about Blue Bird Corporation late last year, investors had access to financial results through the end of the 2024 fiscal year. But results now extend through the first quarter of 2025. For that window of time, things weren't exactly great. Take revenue as an example. During the first quarter, sales totaled $313.9 million. That represents a decline of 1.2% compared to the $317.7 million the business reported one year earlier. This weakness for the company came in spite of the fact that the firm’s Parts segment, which focuses on selling replacement bus parts to customers, reported a rise in revenue from $24.2 million to $25.7 million. That increase of 6.2% was because of higher prices that management put into place in order to combat inflationary pressures. Despite this, the firm also enjoyed higher fulfillment volumes and favorable changes in product and channel mix.

The pain for the company, then, involved its Bus segment. This is the part of the company that, naturally, focuses on the design, production, and sale, of school buses and extended warranties associated with them. Year over year, revenue for this unit fell 1.8% from $293.4 million to $288.1 million. This was in spite of the fact that the number of units booked by the business grew slightly from 2,129 to 2,130. The primary culprit, then, ended up being customer and product mix changes. Ultimately, these pushed revenue down by 1.9% year over year. When it comes to the overall units produced, it is worth noting that management claimed that supply chain constraints reduced the firm's ability to produce and deliver buses in both fiscal quarters. If accurate, this would mean that deliveries likely would have been higher each year had it not been for these problems.

Even though revenue for the company contracted, profitability improved, with net income climbing from $26.2 million to $28.7 million. This is surprising when you consider that the firm's gross profit margin actually contracted marginally because of inflation. This improvement was actually driven by two things. First, the company saw a $1.7 million decline in interest expense year over year. This was because of lower debt outstanding and a decrease in the company's interest rate from 8.5% to 6.4%. Furthermore, during the first quarter of 2025, Blue Bird Corporation sold $2.6 million of state emissions credits that it did not expect to use. It generated nothing in the way of revenue from these the previous year. Excluding this, profitability would have been lower.

Other profitability metrics ended up coming in mixed. Adjusted net profits, fortunately, expanded from $29.7 million to $30.6 million. Operating cash flow surged from $0.2 million to $26.4 million. However, if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $35.9 million to $31.3 million. Furthermore, EBITDA for the business contracted year over year from $47.6 million to $45.8 million.

When it comes to the 2025 fiscal year, management said that they still expect revenue of between $1.4 billion and $1.5 billion. This is based on deliveries of 9,250 units, which would be up from the 9,000 units the company achieved last year. Much of this growth is expected to involve electric units, with that number forecast to decline from 704 units to 1,000 units. The firm did increase the range of what EBITDA should be from previous guidance. They now expect the range to be between $185 million and $215 million. This is $5 million lower on the low end and higher on the high end than what was previously anticipated. If we hit the midpoint here of $200 million, that would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of $135.2 million.

In the chart above, we can see what all of this means for the company from a valuation perspective. All things considered, I think the stock looks quite cheap, particularly on a forward basis. It's not every day that you can buy stock in an industry leader that's growing at a respectable pace, with multiples in the mid to high single digit range. When it comes to the future, management also expects great things. In the image below, you can see what guidance looks like extending out for the next few years. To be clear, this guidance is unchanged from what the company put out for their fourth quarter earnings release.

If everything goes according to plan, then by 2028, the firm should likely be delivering between 11,000 units and 12,000 units per year. That should result in annual revenue of between $1.85 billion and $2 billion. That growth, combined with higher operating margins, should push EBITDA up to between $270 million and $300 million. Considering that this would translate to, at the midpoint, growth of 55.8% over what the company achieved in 2024, I would say that things are looking up.

This is not to say that there aren't certain risks involved. The current Presidential Administration seems to be quite hostile to anything electric. So there could ultimately be certain funding challenges as time goes on. But that's not all. There is broader economic uncertainty because of both political and economic issues, plus, tied into that, is uncertainty regarding tariffs.

In the firm's first quarter earnings release for the 2025 fiscal year, it mentioned that there could be price increases that it would have to charge of up to 5% on all of their non-electric vehicle buses by the end of February had the current Administration followed through on its original tariff threats. Management said that they are actively exploring sourcing and other options with suppliers during this time. But considering that it has already been faced with supply chain issues that curtailed production in the first quarter of this year even, it is unclear what the end result would be.

In my previous article about the firm, I pointed out certain industry conditions that look favorable to the business. Today, throughout the US and Canada, there are over 500,000 school buses in operation that transport 26 million kids each day. This market is dominated by only three players, and Blue Bird Corporation is one of them. Even though school districts are financially strapped and electric buses tend to be expensive compared to traditional ones, there is no doubt that we are moving in the direction of electric vehicles in general. Furthermore, according to the company, over 47% of all 570,000 school buses on the road throughout the US and Canada are 10 years of age or older. Some of them are as old as 35 years.

At present, management expects to see industry deliveries throughout North America grow at an annualized rate of about 6% between 2024 and 2029. Most of the increase from 29,300 deliveries last year to 39,500 deliveries at the end of this window of time is forecasted to occur this year and next year. This year, the firm is forecasting 33,200 deliveries. And in 2026, it expects this number to climb to 36,400.

If everything goes as management expects it to, the growth prospects for shareholders should be encouraging. In the table above, I decided to project out, based on management's guidance and how shares are currently priced, the revenue, EBITDA, and operating cash flow that the company should generate from now through 2028. I also show pricing data for this window of time. For 2028 specifically, I looked at the low end of the range, the midpoint of the range, and the high point of the range, that management offered.

*Annualized Upside Implied

Obviously, the stock gets even cheaper as cash flows grow. But if we assume that the business warrants trading at the same multiples it is at right now, then by 2028, we should expect annualized upside of between 10.1% and 13.2%. Considering that the S&P 500 usually averages between 11% and 12% per annum, it might seem curious that I have decided to stick with a ‘strong buy’ rating. However, this is because I also believe that margin expansion, market share gains, and a realization by the market that current shares are already cheap, will lead to higher trading multiples. If, for instance, the company would be worth 12 times adjusted operating cash flow on a price to adjusted operating cash flow basis, or 10 times EBITDA on an EV to EBITDA basis, this would imply upside of between 17.4% per annum and 27.5% per annum. That is certainly deserving of a very bullish assessment.

Takeaway

As things stand, the market currently disagrees with my prior assessment. However, I firmly believe the market is wrong. Shares are attractively priced and, while recent results have been less than ideal, the overall fundamental health of the business is solid. Growth is almost certain to occur over the next few years, and the low trading multiples that we are talking about suggest that attractive upside is justified. Because of this, I'm going to stick with the ‘strong buy’ rating I had the stock at last November.

data.title: PDF Solutions: Embracing M&A As The Solution
data.description:
Shares of PDF Solutions (NASDAQ:PDFS) have seen whipsaw action in recent days. After plunging on the back of a softer end to 2024, and an apparent disappointing guidance for 2025, the company announced an interesting acquisition, one triggered a recovery in the share price.

While I see the potential in the deal, there are many question marks as it is the lack of realistic profits (for a long time already) which prevent me from getting interested. This is despite the positioning towards interesting, and growing market segments.

A Semiconductor Supplier

PDF Solutions provides AI, data analytics and equipment connectivity solutions for the semiconductor industry. Aiming to improve process efficiency and reliability, the company offers differentiated data, analytics platform and connectivity & control solutions.

The platform starts with leading analytics, supported by great data access & integration across the value chain. What follows is that differentiated data is offered, supported by a unique view of data sources across the value chain.

The shift to analysis of this data results in a strong business model, and enables its customers to deliver on smart manufacturing solutions. The company operates an open platform with links to key software platforms such as Siemens, SAP, AWS and Microsoft, to name a few.

The company has over 350 revenue generating customers which have over 140,000 equipment factory connections, including tens of thousands of process tools. The opportunity is expanding, with PDF focusing on system clients, EMS providers, product companies, factories, and equipment & IP players. The business generates nearly half of sales in the US, complemented by operations in Japan and the rest of the world.

Despite the great talk and recent developments, it should be understood that PDF Solutions has been founded back in 1991 already, as it has been a publicly traded business since 2001. Shares fell to the low single digits around the time of the economic crisis in 2009 as shares have long traded in a $10-$30 range, generally having traded at higher levels, but amidst greater volatility, in recent years.

The Current Stance

Over the past decade, PDF Solutions has seen relatively solid growth. A roughly $100 million business in the period 2015-2020 has seen revenues grow towards $180 million as of today. More important is that the company has improved the bottom-line results from a loss to essentially break-even results.

Despite these advancements, shares have lost half their value from a peak around $45 per share in the summer of 2023, to current levels at $22 per share.

To put this move into perspective, let's first look at the 2023 results, as released in February 2024. Revenues were reported up 12% to nearly $166 million as the company turned a GAAP loss of $3 million and change in 2022 to a profit of $3 million in 2023. Earnings were aided by the existence of a $135 million net cash position (and the net interest income received on this).

Driven by softness in the industry, the company guided for revenues to come in flattish in the first half year of 2024, with revenue growth seen around 20% in the second half of the year. The company largely delivered on this, as revenue growth in the first half of the year was pretty flattish, with third quarter sales up 10% and fourth quarter sales up 22% on the year before.

This made that full-year sales were up 8% to nearly $180 million, as GAAP operating profits for the year were reported at $4 million, essentially all the result of interest income on net cash balances held.

Shares fell from $28 to $23 upon the release of the fourth quarter results in mid-February, granting the 39 million shares a $900 million valuation, including a $115 million net cash position. This resulted in an enterprise valuation just south of $800 million, valuing the business around 4 times sales, and a non-meaningful profit multiple, as profits are not really reported.

Not Inspiring

Given the accelerating momentum in the fourth quarter, the outlook for 2025 is not too convincing. The company guides for full-year sales to advance some 15%, but given the recent growth rates, that suggests a slower pace of growth. Moreover, the company failed to make comments about the margins seen this year, after there has been a lack of margin progress made in 2024.

This comes after 2024 GAAP earnings were only reported at $0.10 per share, and while adjusted earnings were reported at $0.84 per share, the vast majority of the reconciliation was due to stock-based compensation expense, something which I am not happy to adjust for.

A Substantial Deal

Days after the earnings release, the company announced the acquisition of secureWISE, a widely used secure, remote connectivity solutions provider within the semiconductor manufacturing equipment industry. The deal is substantial, as PDF Solutions will pay $130 million to acquire the business, equal to more than 15% of the current enterprise valuation.

Assuming that closing is seen in the first quarter, the company calls out for 21-23% revenue growth this year. A roughly 7% increase in the full year sales guidance (from the previous 15% sales growth guidance) suggests a $13 million revenue contribution during the remainder of the year, or about $18 million per annum. This suggests that quite a steep multiple has been paid, estimated at around 7 times sales.

Few other details were announced in the press release, other than that the deal is set to be accretive to 2025 earnings, without this statement being quantified, although that a more profound (but still non-quantified) impact is seen for 2026 earnings per share.

The deal presentation provided some more background on secureWISE which employs about 40 workers, while more precise financial details are lacking.

A Final Word

Pro forma of the transaction, assuming all the deal is paid for with cash and debt, PDF operates with 39 million shares and a modest net debt load of $15 million.

Despite the relatively hefty price tag, investors have given their blessing to the deal as shares rose some 10% in reaction to the purchase, adding nearly a hundred million to the market value of the firm. I can understand why, because it seems that the purchase could be quite profitable and accretive to all of PDF.

That, however, leaves the problem with the core business, which seems to trade at a reasonable 4 times sales multiple (in combination with 15% growth). The issue, however, is the lack of any substantial and realistic profits here, which makes it hard to get upbeat, and attach a reasonable valuation to the firm.

data.title: Manole Capital: 1st Quarter 2025 Investor Newsletter
data.description:
Introduction:

We hope your 2025 is off to a strong start! Wishing you and your loved ones a year filled with health, prosperity, and success.

This first newsletter of the year is a special one - it marks our 10th anniversary. In this edition, we explore key market and FINTECH developments shaping 2025. On the macro front, we break down major market trends, economic shifts, and expectations for the year, including interest rates, inflation, and the Fed's next moves. We also dive into AI and examine how increased AI spending could impact the Magnificent Seven's free cash flow. Specifically within FINTECH, we highlight the likelihood of proposed credit card price caps (with two very different political partners) and the potential beneficiaries of removing the penny from circulation.

The Market:

January was a robust month for both our portfolios and the broader stock market, with the S&P 500 (SP500) (SPX) gaining +2.7%. Historically, January's performance has been viewed as a meaningful indicator for the rest of the year. Since 1957, the S&P 500 has ended the year in positive territory 72% of the time. However, when January posts a gain, that probability jumps to 88%.

Bloomberg and Canaccord Genuity research and analysis reinforces this trend. When the market has gained more than 20% in the prior year and follows up with a strong January, the S&P 500 has historically delivered another positive year. This pattern has occurred nine times before, and each time, the index finished the calendar year higher, with an average gain exceeding 15%.

Wall Street projects 4.8% revenue growth and 14.8% earnings growth for the S&P 500 in 2024, with Technology once again leading the way. The sector is expected to see 12% revenue growth and an 18% earnings increase. Other notable contributors include Financials (5% revenue growth, 29% earnings growth) and Consumer Discretionary (6% revenue growth, 25% earnings growth). Conversely, the biggest drag on overall market growth is expected to be Energy, where revenue is forecasted to decline by 2% and earnings by 30%.

After multiple years of strong market performance fueled largely by multiple expansion, we believe valuations are now fair to fully valued. As we highlighted in last quarter's newsletter (search for our research on Seeking Alpha), stock performance this year will likely be driven by fundamental revenue and earnings growth rather than further multiple expansion.

Looking Ahead:

Stocks have started strong in 2025, following exceptional runs in 2024 (+23%) and 2023 (+24%). While some investors worry that these back-to-back gains echo the dot-com bubble, we see key differences.

When the bubble burst in 2000, the S&P 500 declined by 10%, fueled by excessive speculation in unprofitable tech companies and the fading Y2K scare. Yet, few would argue that the internet wasn't an era-defining innovation - it permanently reshaped business and global economies. Consider the late 1990s: after surging +34% in 1995, the S&P 500 gained another +20% in 1996. Rather than collapsing after two strong years, the market continued to rise, up +31% in 1997, +27% in 1998, and +20% in 1999 - before a modest, single-year pullback in 2000. This five-year rally demonstrates that strong gains don't necessarily signal an imminent downturn.

Today, many investors believe AI is driving a transformation comparable to the internet boom of the 1990s. Technology stocks are leading the charge, fueling another "tech boom." Will AI revolutionize daily life and reshape business operations? For some industries, absolutely. For others, its impact may be more measured. The key distinction between now and the dot-com era lies in the fundamentals. Back then, many tech companies had little to no revenue, earnings, or cash flow. Today's AI leaders are posting significant growth, backed by real earnings and sustainable business models.

Magnificent Seven:

Over the past few years, we've discussed the growing concentration of the S&P 500 in the Magnificent Seven stocks. After surging higher in both 2023 and 2024, these companies seemed unstoppable. Due to the market capitalization-weighted structure of the S&P 500, these seven stocks now account for roughly 35% of the index's total weight - or nearly $18 trillion in combined market value. To put that in perspective, this is roughly equivalent to the GDP of China.

These stocks have been the primary drivers of the S&P 500's overall performance. Over the past two years, the Magnificent Seven contributed nearly 60% of the index's total gains, with Nvidia (NVDA) alone responsible for over 25% of the S&P 500's return in the last three years.

As highlighted by Goldman Sachs research, over the last two years, these seven companies have generated the vast majority of the index's sales and earnings growth. Meanwhile, the other 493 companies in the S&P 500 saw earnings decline in 2023 and grow just 3% in 2024.

This outperformance has made US markets the dominant force in global equities, now accounting for two-thirds of total world stock market capitalization. Many investors see the US as the only reliable growth engine, particularly as European economies struggle, and China faces mounting economic pressures. Despite reported GDP growth, China's economy appears stressed, and Trump's renewed focus on tariffs could further weigh on its outlook.

While mega-cap tech still dominates, we've seen early signs of market broadening. So far this year, only Meta (META) and Amazon (AMZN) have posted gains, suggesting a potential shift in market leadership. Whether this trend continues remains to be seen, but for investors, a more diversified market would be a welcome development.

A Free Cash Flow Shift:

Following fourth-quarter results and 2025 forecasts, one major theme has emerged for the Magnificent Seven - a shift in free cash flow dynamics. Historically, these companies operated with asset-light models, generating massive cash flow with relatively low capital expenditures. However, that appears to be changing.

On recent earnings calls, nearly all seven companies signaled plans for significant capital spending in 2025, largely tied to AI investments. For example, Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) projects $75 billion in capital expenditures this year and Meta expects to spend $65 billion, a 60% increase year-over-year.

The key question remains: Will billions poured into AI translate into sustainable earnings growth and cash flow? We're not suggesting this is overspending - only company management can determine if these investments will generate a strong return. What's clear is that tech giants are making massive AI investments, which will significantly reduce free cash flow in the short term.

Artificial Intelligence ('AI'):

Our biggest question about AI isn't understanding its applications or the underlying technology - it's distinguishing hype from reality. Over the past few years, AI-related stocks have soared, with many investors believing the only way to generate alpha was to own Nvidia or an AI play.

Then, on January 27th, DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) shook the AI landscape by claiming it had trained a high-performing AI model using older, cheaper chips for just $6 million. This announcement raised serious questions about the massive AI infrastructure investments made by US tech giants and sent shockwaves through the market. Investors began to question and reassess their holdings, leading to a sharp selloff across the sector.

The reaction was swift and severe - tech stocks shed $1 trillion in market value, with Nvidia alone plunging 17% and erasing $657 billion in market cap. For context, only 13 US companies even have a market cap above that amount. As the old market adage goes, "stocks take the escalator up, but the express elevator down." This was a prime example of that dynamic, as investors opted to "sell first and ask questions later."

DeepSeek's $6 million claim is questionable at best. Can we really take China's word at face value? If you need a reminder of why skepticism is warranted, check out DeepSeek's vs. ChatGPT's responses to questions about Tiananmen Square. It is enlightening...

The US remains firmly in the lead when it comes to AI, with American chipmakers and AI firms driving rapid innovation.

Beyond potential exaggerations, key geopolitical factors also raise concerns. The timing of DeepSeek's announcement was particularly interesting, as it came right after the unveiling of a $500 billion AI infrastructure investment plan called The Stargate Project. President Trump, alongside executives from OpenAI, Oracle (ORCL), and SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY), announced this initiative to expand US AI infrastructure, including data centers, AI server racks, and semiconductor capacity. Given the ongoing AI rivalry between the US and China, and the recent Chinese cyberattack on the US Treasury Secretary's computer, this claim feels strategic. Was this truly a breakthrough in AI efficiency, or a calculated move in the ongoing tech war?

However, one major obstacle remains - semiconductor supply. Trump's proposed tariffs of 25%, 50%, or even 100% on imported chips aim to boost domestic semiconductor production, but the reality is that this shift won't happen overnight. Advanced chip fabs take 4+ years and $10 to $20 billion to build, and the technical expertise required is extremely specialized. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) remains the only foundry capable of producing the most advanced AI chips, supplying Nvidia, Apple, and virtually every major AI player. While the CHIPS Act has incentivized TSMC to build plants in Arizona, they won't be fully operational until 2030, and even then, they will represent only a fraction of global demand.

The AI boom has been fueled by speculation, uncertainty, and strong opinions - often with little hard data. While DeepSeek's claim may have rattled the market, we view the selloff as more of a panic reaction than a fundamental threat. If anything, it serves as a wake-up call for AI leaders to refine their capital allocation strategies.

Did DeepSeek (and China) just turn on the lights, stop the music, and take away the punch bowl at the AI party? We don't think so. But it certainly caught the market off guard - like a 1986 Mike Tyson left hook.

The Fed, Interest Rates and Inflation:

Disinflation - a slowdown in the rate of inflation - is not the same as deflation, which refers to an outright decline in prices. While deflationary pressures emerged in mid-2022, they began to subside by the summer of 2023. However, recent economic reports suggest inflation remains persistent, and disinflation isn't progressing as quickly as the Federal Reserve would like.

To gauge market expectations for interest rate movements, we often turn to the CME's Interest Rate tool. As this link shows, the market currently expects two 25-basis-point rate cuts in 2025. However, traders are increasingly skeptical - the probability of no rate cuts this year has risen to 30%, up from 20% earlier this week. Many economists now believe that, unless inflation starts easing meaningfully, the Fed could hold rates steady between 4.25% and 4.5% through the summer.

During his semiannual monetary policy testimony on Capitol Hill, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell reinforced this cautious stance. He said, "With our policy stance now significantly less restrictive than it had been and the economy remaining strong, we do not need to be in a hurry to adjust our policy stance." He further emphasized the Fed's measured approach to potential rate cuts by stating, "If the economy remains strong and inflation does not continue to move sustainably toward 2%, we can maintain policy restraint for longer."

Key takeaways from Chairman Powell's testimony:

  • The Fed is in no rush to cut interest rates.
  • He urged lawmakers to address government debt and reduce the deficit.
  • He acknowledged that proposed tariffs could worsen inflation.
  • He ruled out the creation of a Fed-issued digital currency.
  • He defended his Fed staff, stating they were "overworked," not overstaffed.

Despite ongoing calls from President Trump for lower interest rates, the Fed remains in a "wait-and-see" mode. The Fed remains data-dependent, prioritizing inflation trends over political pressure. The latest Consumer Price Index (CPI) report from the Bureau of Labor Statistics reinforced this cautious stance. January CPI came in at 3.0% year-over-year, 20 basis points above expectations, while the month-over-month increase of 0.5% was also higher than forecast. This marks the biggest monthly gain since August 2023. Much of the increase was driven by higher food costs, with egg prices soaring 53% year-over-year. Meanwhile, Core CPI (which excludes volatile food and energy prices) rose 3.3% year-over-year and 0.4% month-over-month. Shelter costs, which account for nearly 30% of the inflation index, remained a key driver, rising 4.4% year-over-year.

As we've noted before, the "last mile" of inflation - getting it down to the Fed's 2% target - remains the most difficult phase of rate policy adjustments. Unless inflation declines further, the Fed is likely to remain on hold.

Price Caps & Consumer Spending:

On the campaign trail, candidate Trump floated the idea of capping credit card interest rates at 10%. Then, in an unusual bipartisan move, independent Senator Bernie Sanders ('VT') and conservative Republican Senator Josh Hawley ('MO') introduced legislation on February 4th proposing similar restrictions. While Washington is undergoing significant shifts, one thing remains certain - this bill is unrealistic and unlikely to become law.

The interest in this issue is understandable, given that average credit card APRs have surged to an all-time high of 28.6%. It's rare to see bipartisan agreement, especially from these two polar opposites, and their effort to provide relief to working families burdened by debt is commendable. While their statements resonate with many Americans, the economic reality is far more complex. Total US credit card debt has now surpassed $1.2 trillion and continues to rise. According to the Fed, 30-day delinquencies rose by 10% to 3.52%, signaling growing financial strain on consumers. In addition, the number of consumers making only the minimum payment climbed to 10.75%.

As Senator Sanders said, "When large financial institutions charge over 25 percent interest on credit cards, they are not engaged in the business of making credit available. They are engaged in extortion and loan sharking. We cannot continue to allow big banks to make huge profits ripping off the American people." Senator Hawley echoed this frustration and stated, "Working Americans are drowning in record credit card debt while the biggest credit card issuers get richer and richer by hiking their interest rates to the moon. It's not just wrong, it's exploitative. And it needs to end."

While the Sanders-Hawley bill may make for compelling political rhetoric, the reality is that price controls rarely work as intended. If banks determine that credit cards are no longer a profitable product under such constraints, they will simply pull back from issuing them. As the American Bankers Association (ABA) warned, "This proposal would result in the loss of credit access for the very consumers who need it the most, forcing them to use less-regulated, more risky alternatives, including payday lenders and loan sharks." JPMorgan Chase, one of the nation's largest credit card issuers, pointed to a Consumer Bankers Association (CBA) study and labeled the legislation as "misguided." We believe that imposing government-mandated price caps on interest rates would likely backfire - severely limiting credit access and shrinking consumer spending power. Instead of lowering costs for consumers, they could lead to reduced credit access, pushing those in need toward even riskier alternatives.

The Penny:

We may soon be seeing a lot less of President Lincoln's right cheekbone. President Trump recently signed an executive order directing the Treasury to stop minting pennies, calling them "wasteful".

Eliminating the penny isn't a new idea. The debate has resurfaced multiple times over the years, with support from federal officials, lawmakers, and even President Obama. If you have time, check out Caity Weaver's 7,000-word deep dive on banning the penny - it's worth the read.

The US Mint reported a $85.3 million loss last fiscal year producing billions of pennies, which currently cost 3.7 cents each to manufacture. And it's not just pennies - nickels are also more expensive to produce than they're worth.

Not everyone is eager to say goodbye to the penny. Companies like Artazn, which has made over $1 billion supplying zinc to the US Mint, and Coinstar, the operator of coin-cashing machines, have a vested interest in keeping pennies in circulation.

But if the penny disappears, who stands to gain? Our portfolio is heavily weighted toward digital payment beneficiaries, and a move away from cash could further accelerate their growth. Businesses may begin rounding prices up to the nearest nickel, which could lead to a modest increase in prices or a form of small-scale re-inflation. More importantly, it could accelerate the ongoing shift toward digital payments, further reducing the role of physical cash.

The transition from cash to digital payments was already well underway, but it accelerated significantly during COVID. Last year, Zelle processed over $1 trillion in payments, solidifying its position as a major player in digital transactions. Launched in 2017 and operated by Early Warning Services (EWS), Zelle is backed by major banks and was designed to compete with Venmo (PayPal) and Cash App (Block/Square) in the P2P and A2A payment space.

If you've ever used Venmo, Cash App, or Zelle to settle a golf bet, pay a babysitter, or split a dinner bill, you're part of this shift. And it's not just Gen Z driving adoption - older generations are increasingly using their banking apps to simplify payments. Last year, Zelle's user base grew 12% to 151 million accounts, reflecting the ongoing shift away from cash toward digital payments.

Conclusion:

Inflation remains a concern, geopolitical risks persist, and mounting deficits and national debt loom on the horizon. However, we remain focused and disciplined, resisting the temptation to chase rallies or deviate from our strategy. History has shown that markets thrive despite their "wall of worry," often using uncertainty as fuel for growth.

Despite ongoing challenges, the US economy remains the envy of the world, driven by resilience, innovation, and ingenuity. We anticipate a healthy market environment and a resurgence in M&A and IPO activity, though periods of uncertainty and volatility will likely persist.

We anticipate that the Fed will lower interest rates gradually, preserving its policy tools for future economic challenges. Rather than speculating on rate changes, our priority remains delivering value and generating alpha for our clients. Our philosophy is unchanged: focus on long-term growth, free cash flow, and staying prepared for the unexpected - regardless of short-term volatility.

Manole Capital is officially celebrating 10 years, and we wouldn't be here without your trust and loyalty. Looking ahead, we remain committed to our vision of FINTECH, providing access to exciting opportunities in this dynamic and rapidly evolving space. Please reach out anytime - we value our relationship and are always here to chat.

Warren Fisher, CFA

Founder & CEO | Manole Capital Management |warren@manolecapital.com

data.title: Hims & Hers: A Glorious Opportunity To Trim (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
In October 2023, we wrote our first bullish article on Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS).

Titled “Hims & Hers: 3 Reasons To Buy This Potential Growth Stock,” the piece focused on a few key dynamics within the company that, we thought, could power long-term outperformance for shareholders.

At that point, the stock was trading at roughly ~$6, and there was no clear indication that we were right about the 'startup' D2C healthcare company.

Since then, we've followed up with two more “Strong Buy” ratings for HIMS, mostly on the back of the firm's strong growth and margins, along with the attractive-looking valuation:

  • Hims & Hers: Turning The Profitability Corner And Returning 28%.
  • Hims & Hers Is Unbelievably Cheap And Profitable: Buy Now.

In that time, the company has expanded into a much bigger player and grown financials at a rapid rate, even running their first-ever Super Bowl commercial a few weeks ago.

Since writing on Seeking Alpha, this has probably been our best call ever, with the stock up more than 1,000% since our first rating:

All of this is to say that we're quite familiar with the company's “bull case” — mostly because we've been banging on about it now for more than a year.

And yet, despite our bullishness, the company's recent rally of more than 185% year-to-date has begun to stink of “mania.” Sure, organic performance has been excellent, but the valuation is beginning to stretch, and the recent short squeeze appears to be a good opportunity, broadly, to take some chips off the table.

Today, we'll explore HIMS' rally, cover the company's recent financial performance, and explain why we think now — on balance — is a good time to take some profits in this high-flying consumer healthcare stock. At the very least, if you're looking to build a new position, waiting for lower prices seems optimal.

Sound good? Let's dive in.

HIMS Financials

As always, we'll begin with HIMS' financials.

If you haven't read our earlier articles, then it's worth mentioning why we're so bullish on this D2C healthcare company before explaining why we're at a good price to begin taking some profits.

In short, HIMS is a company that sells high-margin, sticky consumer products in the healthcare space. Labeling it as a 'healthcare' company seems like a bit of a mischaracterization, given that very few healthcare firms are fast-moving and consumer-oriented.

Either way, the firm's business model is ideal because it has three key dynamics that we look for in winning stocks, including high gross margins, recurring revenue, and emotional demand. When combined, these three factors make growing revenues almost a foregone conclusion.

Altogether, HIMS has two main limiting factors — sourcing products, and getting new customers in the door.

This is why the company has recently fortified their supply chain and rolled out some new products, including in the weight management and blood testing categories. A larger offering of better products equals more cross-selling opportunities and LTV:

It's also why HIMS is willing to pay so much to acquire new customers.

All in all, one could readily compare HIMS's core economics to a SaaS company, as LTV and CAC are the most important units to look at when it comes to top and bottom-line growth for shareholders.

So, in our view, HIMS has one of the best business models around. But how have the financials been?

In short, HIMS has been crushing it on the financial side. Recent earnings have been great, with a massive beat in Q3 of 2024:

Not only that, but the company's YOY top-line growth has actually re-accelerated to >70%, which is incredible. Typically, growth slows down as markets become more saturated and the revenue base gets bigger, but new weight loss products and broader consumer awareness of HIMS have actually caused growth to pick up, even from this “higher” bar.

This high growth has been facilitated by product launches, but also by incredibly high TTM gross margins, which has allowed management to spend aggressively on customer acquisition:

This, in turn, brings more recurring revenue streams into the business, which powers more consumer acquisition spend, and the flywheel continues.

Eventually, we expect the market will saturate, and CACs will go so high that they become uneconomical. However, when that day comes, HIMS will have built an incredibly high-margin, recurring revenue business with strong FCF generation and multiple levers for further growth.

Looking forward, in our last article, we put out the following model for HIMS's margins this year and next, which shows just how much we expect profitability to inflect through zero and move higher in the next 24 months:

For this calculation, we increased users by 25% YoY (which is a 1,000 basis point decay YoY), alongside an 8% increase in RPU (in line with the historical rate). Finally, we increased OpEx by 25% YoY (decaying more slowly than in the mid-year).

This gets us to about $665 million in net income:

However, as the company's revenue growth rates remain in the >70% range — and considering the EPS blowout we saw in Q3 — we're updating these results to reflect the potential growth we'll see from new product launches, like blood testing.

Looking forward, we see the potential for HIMS to produce ~$500 million in operating income over the NTM, and ~$750 million in operating income in FY '26.

HIMS's Valuation

Here's the thing.

At last writing, we argued that $400 million and $665 million of operating income in 2025 and 2026 would make the multiple — at that time of writing — around 5-8x, which seemed very cheap for the high-quality revenue streams driving results:

$400 million would put the $3.5 billion valuation in the fall of next year at about 8.82x operating income. Because of the few adjustments HIMS makes from operating income to net income, aside from tax, this could very nearly be about a 9x P/E...

At this price [for NNTM], you're looking at around 5.3x net income, and HIMS would have also paid back roughly 29% of the company's market cap by that period, an incredible earnings yield of about 17% per annum.

Now, HIMS's stock price has appreciated by more than 300%, and the market cap is closer to ~$15 billion.

Even if you take our expanded projections into account, you're looking at an NTM P/OI of 30x, and a NNTM P/OI of roughly 20x. For simplicity purposes, factoring in taxes and other items, you could be looking at a much more expensive overall P/E of ~35x, and then sequentially ~26x in 2026.

We're bullish on HIMS, but the valuation has rapidly expanded, which has inversely reduced the attractiveness of the stock.

Also complicating matters is the recent price action, which has been hectic.

The most recent short interest reading for HIMS stands at 27%, which is quite high (anything above 10% should raise an eyebrow).

Thus, while multiple bullish catalysts — like the Super Bowl commercial and the Trybe acquisition — have boosted sentiment, the rallies have been largely exacerbated by shorts blowing out in the stock.

This has led to the largest-ever deviation of HIMS pricing from the average pricing, which makes this, in our view, an extremely interesting time to consider dumping shares:

It's true that short squeezes are notoriously difficult to “time.”

That said, getting 26x '26 P/E for this stock, combined with the underlying squeeze, seems like a good price to take some chips off the table.

Summary

Even if HIMS stock ultimately continues higher, selling a chunk of your position now and waiting for a lower price to buy back in — perhaps in the $40s — appears to be the best way to play the stock.

Some “investor” types may argue that fiddling with a winner can mean losing out on long-run gains. However, in our view, when the market presents you with a squeeze like this, trimming and taking advantage is the optimal thing to do.

If you're looking to build a new position, then waiting for lower prices and getting a better multiple also appears optimal.

In both cases, we feel confident that buyers will be able to snag a lower price than the current one at some point over the next year.

Thus, our downgrade to a “Hold” rating.

Stay safe out there!
